questions.prompt: A recursive function has correct O(n) time complexity but crashes with StackOverflowError on large input. What's the accurate explanation?

options.text: Recursion depth is bounded by the call stack's fixed, comparatively small memory region; O(n) time complexity says nothing about whether n simultaneous stack frames will fit in that region.
This means the algorithm's time complexity was miscalculated and is actually exponential.
StackOverflowError only occurs due to infinite loops, so there must be a missing base case.
This is a JVM bug that should be reported.

questions.explanation: Time complexity and stack depth are separate concerns: an algorithm can be provably O(n) time while still requiring O(n) simultaneously-alive stack frames, which is a memory constraint the call stack's fixed size (not the heap) enforces. (b) time complexity and crash cause are unrelated here. (c) a StackOverflowError can occur with a correct base case if recursion depth alone is too large for the stack — no infinite loop required. (d) this is expected, documented JVM behavior, not a bug.

questions.prompt: A teammate rewrites a deeply recursive Java function into 'tail-recursive' form, expecting this to fix stack overflow on large input. Will it?

options.text: No — unlike some functional languages, the JVM does not perform tail-call elimination, so a tail-recursive Java function still consumes one stack frame per call and can still overflow.
Yes — all modern JVMs automatically optimize tail-recursive calls into loops.
Yes, but only if the method is marked with the @TailRecursive annotation.
It depends on whether the JIT compiler has warmed up.

questions.explanation: This is a specific, useful fact: Java (unlike Scala with @tailrec-verified optimization, or languages with mandated tail-call elimination) does not eliminate tail calls at the bytecode or JIT level — every call, tail-positioned or not, still pushes a frame. The real fix is converting to an explicit loop with your own heap-backed stack (e.g. ArrayDeque). (b) and (c) describe behavior Java does not have — no such annotation or automatic optimization exists in standard Java. (d) JIT warm-up affects speed, not stack frame allocation for recursive calls.

questions.prompt: In modern Java, what is the recommended default class for stack or queue usage, and why not java.util.Stack or LinkedList?

options.text: ArrayDeque — java.util.Stack is a legacy synchronized Vector subclass (unnecessary locking overhead, inconsistent API), and LinkedList has worse cache locality for the sequential end-only access that stacks/queues perform.
java.util.Stack, because it is specifically designed and optimized for stack operations.
LinkedList, because its name indicates it is the intended structure for sequential access patterns.
There is no meaningful difference between the three; any is an equally good default.

questions.explanation: This connects directly to chapters 1 and 3: ArrayDeque's contiguous backing array wins on cache locality, and it avoids java.util.Stack's needless synchronization (a holdover from Java's earliest collection classes, predating the modern Collections Framework). (b) is the misleading-name trap the chapter calls out — Stack is legacy baggage, not the recommended tool. (c) LinkedList's name is equally misleading here — 'linked' describes its internal structure, not that it's ideal for every 'list-like' use case. (d) there is a measurable, real difference in both correctness-adjacent behavior (unwanted synchronization) and performance.

questions.prompt: Why does this 'next greater element' implementation run in O(n) total time despite the nested while loop, rather than O(n^2)?

options.text: Each index is pushed onto the stack exactly once and popped at most once across the entire run, so total while-loop iterations over all outer-loop steps is bounded by n, not n per outer step.
The JIT compiler automatically detects and optimizes nested loops with this shape into O(n).
It is actually O(n^2) in the worst case; the O(n) claim is a common misconception.
It's O(n) only because the array is assumed to be sorted beforehand.

questions.explanation: This is the amortized-cost argument behind every monotonic stack: bound the TOTAL work across the whole run, not the worst case of a single iteration — since every element enters the stack once and leaves at most once, total push+pop operations across all iterations is O(n), not O(n) per outer iteration. (b) no such general JIT optimization exists; the O(n) bound is an algorithmic property, not a compiler trick. (c) is incorrect — this is a textbook-verified O(n) technique, precisely because of the amortized argument in (a). (d) there is no sortedness assumption or requirement anywhere in this algorithm.

questions.prompt: Which problem phrasing is the clearest signal to reach for a monotonic stack rather than a nested loop?

options.text: "For each day, find the number of days until a warmer temperature" (a 'next greater element'-shaped problem).
"Sort an array of temperatures in ascending order."
"Find the sum of all temperatures in the array."
"Reverse the array of temperatures in place."

questions.explanation: This is a direct instance of the 'next greater element' pattern (find, for each position, the nearest future element satisfying a comparison) — the exact shape a monotonic stack collapses from O(n^2) to O(n). (b), (c), (d) are all well-served by simpler, standard techniques (sorting, a single accumulation pass, in-place index swapping) with no monotonic-stack structure to exploit.

questions.prompt: The 'sliding window maximum' problem (max of every k-sized window as it slides across an array) can be solved in O(n) total using a deque holding candidate indices. What is the key invariant that makes this work?

options.text: The deque is kept in decreasing value order: before pushing a new index, any indices at the back with smaller values are popped (they can never be the max again while the new, larger element is in range), and expired out-of-window indices are popped from the front.
The deque stores every element seen so far, and the maximum is recomputed by scanning the whole deque for each window.
The array must be sorted first, after which a simple two-pointer scan finds each window's maximum.
The deque holds only the current window's elements in their original order, and the front element is always assumed to be the maximum.

questions.explanation: Maintaining decreasing order (evicting dominated smaller values from the back) means the front of the deque is always the current window's maximum without ever rescanning — each index is still pushed once and popped at most once total, giving the same amortized O(n) bound as any monotonic structure. (b) rescanning per window reintroduces O(n*k) cost, defeating the purpose. (c) sorting destroys the positional/window information needed and isn't part of this technique. (d) the front being 'assumed' the max without the decreasing-order invariant would be wrong — the invariant is what guarantees it, not the mere position in the deque.
